summary: Mechanical include-path cleanup across 152 files: short header includes such as `#include <rand.h>`, `#include <ftfox.h>`, and `#include <ftdonkey.h>` were replaced with repository-root-relative angle-bracket includes like `#include <MSL/rand.h>` and `#include <melee/ft/chara/ftFox/ftfox.h>`. The PR body says this fixes context generation, so the value was tooling reliability and unambiguous header resolution rather than codegen or behavior changes.
what_changed: Converted local-looking angle includes to fully qualified project paths throughout the source tree.
Examples from the diff include `#include <rand.h>` to `#include <MSL/rand.h>`, `#include <mem_TRK.h>` to `#include <MetroTRK/mem_TRK.h>`, and `#include <__mem.h>` to `#include <Runtime/__mem.h>`.

Fighter character implementation files now include their character headers by full tree path, for example `#include <ftfox.h>` became `#include <melee/ft/chara/ftFox/ftfox.h>` and `#include <ftdonkey.h>` became `#include <melee/ft/chara/ftDonkey/ftdonkey.h>`.
The change was broad but intentionally content-neutral: the PR reports 157 added and 157 deleted lines, almost entirely one-for-one include replacements.
The PR body states the motivation: `Fixes context generation.` No semantic C code changes or matching-specific changes are evident in the provided diff excerpt.
smart_moves: Used a repository-root-relative include convention instead of relying on basename-only headers, reducing ambiguity when many subsystems have similarly named files.
Kept the patch mechanical and reviewable: one-line include substitutions across many files with balanced additions/deletions.
Applied the convention consistently across third-party/runtime-like areas such as MSL, MetroTRK, and Runtime as well as Melee gameplay folders.
Preserved angle-bracket include style while making the path explicit, matching the PR title's stated convention: `#include <path/to/file.h>`.
decomp_lessons: For decomp repositories with generated context, include paths should be deterministic and rooted in the project include layout; basename-only includes can break or confuse context generation.
Large mechanical include migrations are safest when they avoid mixing in functional code changes.
Header path normalization can be a tooling fix even when compiled code behavior is unchanged.
When adding new C files, prefer full project paths for local project headers, especially under duplicated patterns like `melee/ft/chara/<character>/<header>.h`.
naming_conventions: Use angle-bracket includes with explicit project-relative paths: `#include <path/to/file.h>`.
Library/runtime headers were qualified by top-level directory, e.g. `MSL/rand.h`, `MetroTRK/mem_TRK.h`, and `Runtime/__mem.h`.
Fighter character headers followed `melee/ft/chara/<CharacterDir>/<header>.h`, preserving exact directory casing such as `ftFox`, `ftGameWatch`, `ftGigaKoopa`, and `ftIceClimber`.
Header basenames remained unchanged; the convention changed the include path, not symbol or file naming.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: No assembly rewrite or code matching tactic is evidenced. This was a source organization/tooling cleanup.
The likely matching relevance is indirect: stable include resolution helps generate accurate decomp contexts, but the PR excerpt does not show any function-level matching work.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or reviews are present in the provided PR context.
No reviewer rationale beyond the PR body phrase `Fixes context generation` is available.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that add new files and verify they use full project-relative angle includes.
Search for remaining `#include <[A-Za-z0-9_]*.h>` basename-only project includes after PR 532.
Check context generation tooling changes around September 2022 to understand exactly why basename includes failed.
Look for follow-up include cleanup PRs touching headers rather than C source files.
